task.domains: (topic task) (topic hi-d) (topic ui-d) (topic sw-d) (topic indexing-d) (topic ut-d) a(base ishcondition) a(base varid) a(base varref) a(base cid) a(base partslist) (topic oce-d) (topic sdlVariables-c) (topic oceCommonElements-c) (topic oceMetaDecl-c) (topic oceTopic-c) (topic oceTopic-c task oceTask-c) (topic oceTopic-c hi-d oceHighlight-c) (topic oceTopic-c ui-d oceUi-c) (topic oceTopic-c sw-d oceSoftware-c)
task.title: Usage of functional logging

section.title: Introduction
section.p: The creation of an image on media in the Colorado starts by loading media in the drawer.
The media is transported towards the platen via the buffer plate. (This plate is used to decouple the forces/movement of the media in the drawer and the platen.)
The X-transport pinch transports the media over the platen using a controlled media step.
These steps are measured by the M&C-scanner using the AAC-markers.
After the media stops moving the printheads are moved over the media to create a small part of the image.
These steps are repeated until the complete image has been generated.
The settings of the media are stored in the profile.
In the Colorado platform measurements of all kind of sensors are stored in the functional logging.
A part of this data can be used to diagnose print quality related issues. In the table below the most important logging are summarized.

li.p: This monitors the performance of the buffer plate based on two sensors.
The buffer plate is used to guide the media from the drawer/Jumbo roll to the platen.
Using AAC-markers on the media the engine is able to set the correct media steps on the platen.
The data consists of the location of each AAC-marker related to the expected position on the AAC-marker.
After each swath the performance of the printhead is measured using PAINt.
This data can be used to determine the status of each nozzle during printing.
Each print job has unique characteristics like media type, profile settings and ink usage.
All generic information regarding a print job is shown in ECID-information.
The Colorado ink has an expiry date.
If the ink expires unexpected issues can arise.
This data is based on the calculation of the expiry date in the ink tanks.

section.title: Buffer plate
section.p: The buffer plate performs 2 important functions related to print quality. (See also : 15 The function of the buffer).

li.p: Compensate speed differences between roll drive in the media-drawer and the X-transport drive.
Correct steering problems in the media feed.

section.p: The performance of the buffer plate is monitored by two sensors (XMS_BUF_RIGHT_SE and XMS_BUF_LEFT_SE).
These sensors register the position of the left and right side of the buffer plate.
When the buffer plate moves inwards the value of the sensors increases and the roll drive will speed up to feed more media.
If the buffer plate moves outwards the roll drive in the drawer will slow down to feed less media.
The values of these sensors are stored in the functional logging.
Distortion of the values indicate media issues before the X-transport drive (eg : Telescoping of media in the drawer).
Example:
In the graph above an example is shown of the buffer plate logging.
The values of the sensors are plotted on the Y-axis (0-255).
The Blue line represent the left-sensor (or the left side of the media) and the Orange line represents the right-sensor (Right side of the media).
In the example above the Orange and Blue lines are moving parallel which indicates the media is pulled evenly from the drawer.
The absolute difference between the Orange and Blue lines can be corrected by 15-6-018 Check buffer plate [Special Tests].

The values of the buffer plate sensors don’t have a direct relation with the visibility of the media step errors, but the performance of the buffer plate can point to a root cause of media step errors before the X-transport pinch.

section.title: Media step performance
section.p: The X-motion system of the media is handled by the media step control function. (see also : 15 The function of media step control).
This control function uses Automatic Advance Correction (AAC-markers) in combination with the M&C-scanner to determine the position of the media on the platen.
Based on this information the needed media step correction is determined.
The AAC-markers (Normal or Enhanced) are located on the left and right side of the printed image (See example below).
The position of the markers in relation to the expected position is registered in the FLOG-data.

note.p: Graphs created on these data are only showing the difference between the real position of these markers compared to the predicted position.
This means an user-defined deviation of the media-step like “Media step correction factor” will not be seen in these graphs.

section.p: Besides the media step errors the output of the media step controller is also registered.
The value of this output is an indication of the amount of correction which is needed to get the right media steps.
A large value of this correction factor is an indication of a mismatch between the internal media calibration data and the real performance of the media on the platen.
A large value doesn’t mean the media step errors will be seen on the printed image.
As mentioned it is only an indication of the performance of the media step function itself.
If the media is calibrated correctly and the condition of the platen is not changed the output of the media step control function is just several µm to zero.
Example:
In the graph above an example of the media step performance is shown of a 3 meter long print which is printed from the leading edge.
As the X-axis show the relative value compared to the leading edge, the most left value on the X-axis is in this case 0.
In case the most left value isn’t 0 this means the print isn’t made directly from the leading edge, but made without cutting the last job.
On the Y-axis the media step errors are shown in two colors (Blue and Orange) which are the left and right side of the printed image.
As can be seen the values on both sides initially starts at approx. +50 µm (Yellow zone).
As the error has a positive value the expected markers are further away than expected and might result in white lines.
In case of a negative value dark lines might be excepted as the media steps will overlap.
When the media step errors are within +/- 30 µm no deviations on the printed image are expected (green zone).
Media step errors between 30 and 100 µm might be visible (Yellow zone) depending of the used media.
Visibility of the media step errors on highly structured media as Canvas is less compared to smooth Self-Adhesive Media.
In case the errors are above 100 µm the media step errors will surely be seen (Red zone).
In the example above it can be seen the media step controller does not make any corrections until 50 cm after the leading edge.
During this period the media is not covering all zones of the platen.
After 50 cm the media step controller starts correcting the media step errors.
Within a few swaths the Orange and Blue lines are steered towards the green area.
In this case the media step controllers needs to correct every swath approx. 100 µm.
In this particular example the media is not calibrated well.
This can be concluded from the fact media step errors are only visible in the first 50 cm after the leading edge when the media controller is not working yet.
In case of a good calibration the media steps in the first 50 cm are predicted well and should already be within +/- 30 µm.
The second clue of a “bad” calibration is the fact the correction factor is above 100 µm.

section.title: PH performance
section.p: The quality of the printheads is monitored after every swath using the PAINt-algorithm (see also: 04 The process of the Nozzle Failure detection, Nozzle Failure Compensation and PAINt).
This algorithm can detect several causes of failing nozzles (eg. Air).
Based on these causes a nozzle can be classified as “Use”, ”Avoid” or “Don’t use”.
When a nozzle is classified as “Don’t use” it will not be used during the rest of the print.
This means this nozzle needs to be compensated by NFC.
Nozzles which are classified as “Avoid” aren’t used unless there are no other options left.
Besides this dynamic behavior of NFC the amount of marked nozzles during the “Nozzle optimalisation” procedure also contribute to the final result of the print quality.
Of course there is a relationship between the amount of failing nozzles and the final print quality. However this is not a strict relationship.
For example:

li.p: A failing black nozzle might be compensated by another black nozzle in the next swath or even by CMY nozzles.
A failing nozzle in yellow is less visible in print quality comparing to a black failure.
Multiple failing nozzles next to each other are harder to compensate compared to the same amount of separated nozzles.
Not all nozzles might be needed in the next swath
Multi pass print modes are more likely to compensate failing nozzles.

section.p: Given these dependencies there cannot be given a hard number of failing nozzles which will result in print quality issues.

p.b: No printhead may be replaced solitary based on printhead performance graphs.
These graphs are only to be used as support for an already suspected quality issue.

section.p: There are two different graphs which represent the printhead performance (Printhead score and nozzle performance).
The performance of the printheads is measured on an arbitrary scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the best performance and 0 the worst performance.
This score can be used to get an overall impression of the performance of the printheads (In case further details are required the “Nozzle performance” can be used).
As already mentioned the resulting print quality depends of many factors so this score cannot be used as an absolute criteria for a good or bad printhead.
Of course a lower score has more likelihood to result in low print quality.
In case a customer is not complaining about a specific color there is no reason the replace a printhead with a lower score.
This graph should be used to find specific events which might resulted in low print quality.
Example:
The graph above shows the printhead score of every printhead over a specific timeframe.
Every printhead has its own colored line.
In the example above an event happened in June 2022 on Y8 (light yellow line) and another event in October 2022 in K4 (black line).
Both events caused the printhead score to drop permanently for both printheads (Deeper analyses shows that in this case a major media crash caused a loose nozzleplate).

note.p: When not refreshed UVgel ink can dry out at the nozzles. This is mainly observed in the white-printhead of the CMxx-series.
This happens when a customer is printing CMYK instead of CMYK-W.
As a result of the dryed out nozzles the printhead score of the White-printhead will drastically drop. (see screenshot below).
However the CMxx is capable of detecting this issue and will recover the white-printhead before the next CYMKW-print.
This will result in a unstable score. However during printing everything will be according to expectations

section.p: Nozzle performance: This complex graph shows the performance of each nozzle of every printhead after every swath during a print. This graph can be used to determine the rootcause in case of a low printhead score .
The nozzle performance is a 2D representation of the nozzle performance after every swath during a print.
On the vertical-axis the SwathID is shown.
The first swath of a print has SwathID 0 and the last swithID is depended of the length of print.
This can vary between 10 to thousands.
On the horizontal axis every nozzleID is shown.
The first nozzle of the 1st printhead has NozzleID 1 and is shown on the left side of the graph.
A printhead in the Colorado platform has 2 array’s which numbering continues.
This means in case an array has 256 nozzles the 1st nozzle of the second array has nozzleID 257.
Every printhead has a unique color and is represented in that way in the background.
In case a nozzle is deviating during a print this deviation is plotted as a different color.
Every type of deviation has an unique color.
For example: Air at the nozzle (AN) has a blue color as and electrical Open Circuit (OC) has a Yellow color.
In the graph below an example of the nozzle performance graph is shown.
As can be seen one nozzle in the Cyan printhead (C2) is failing on classification DNJN between swathID 200 and 300.
As the nozzleID is 382 this means it is the 1st nozzle of the second array of C2.
In Y8 a nozzle is permanently classified as DNJN.

note.p: As mentioned this graph should only be used for pattern recognition to find root causes of failures.
The absolute amount of failing nozzles and the classifications should not be used as criteria to replace a printhead.

section.p: Besides this 2D representation there is also a graph showing the absolute position of the failing nozzles during a specified swath.
The example above shows 4 printheads from the C1630.
As can be seen 1 printhead consists of 2 MEMS-chips which have 2 arrays.
In this example there is a cluster of Open Circuits (OC) in the Magenta printhead.

section.title: ECID information
section.p: Every print job which is send to the Colorado printer has unique properties.
The most important properties have been summarized in the ECID information.
This information contains 5 tables.

li.p: This part shows the unique properties of the print like timestamp and ECID.
All information regarding the selected media is shown in this table.
However there can be a difference between the selected media and the media on the platen.
The engine cannot detect the real media on the platen.
The only information stored is the media which has been selected by the customer in the RIP.
Properties set in the RIP-profile are shown in this table.
The settings of the job like requested print quality and output settings are shown in this table.
Job Content :
Detailed information regarding the content of the job are shown in this table.
The exact content of an image is NOT stored or logged.

section.p: Example:

section.title: Ink Expiry date
section.p: The UVgel ink has an expiry date like all other UV inks.
The ink will slowly change its properties when aging.
Light and heat will increase the aging effect.
Under normal transport and storage conditions the ink is good to use for one year after production (see also 02 Ink expiration).
After that time, the ink properties might have changed so that print quality cannot be guaranteed anymore.
The expiry date of the ink bottle is programmed into the RFID chip of the bottle.
For the ink inside the printer, an expiry date is calculated and shown on the operator panel.
There is one expiry date for each color.
The expiry date of the ink inside the printer is a weighted average of the current ink in the jar + the newly added bottle.
Every 45 second the expiry date of the ink inside the ink tank is calculated.
Example:
The graph shows 4 different lines which represent the remaining expiry date in days(1 for each color).
If ink is expired the value of the remaining expiry date will be below zero. (Red area in the graph).
The slope of these lines is equal to -1.
This means every day the remaining expiry date will decrease by 1 day.
When the ink tank is refilled by a new ink bottle the line will make a steep step upwards.
The size of this step is depended of the amount of ink before refilling and the remaining shelf life of the ink bottle.
A small step means the remaining expiry date of the new ink bottle is almost the same as the remaining expiry date in the ink tank.
If the remaining expiry date is below 0 warning 290020 will be given and a message on the UI will be presented.
When the ink has been refilled using a new ink bottle the remaining expiry date will be above 0 again, but this event is not logged in the engine.
